question: Can ROCm, Rust, and HIP materially speed up the current slow G1 residual-gate push?

followup32.assessment: A live but tiny descent was obtained. Most wall time is residual-only replay for finite-difference JVP columns and alpha candidates.

followup34.assessment: Wider local support increased runtime and did not descend, so repeated CPU support widening is not the right next acceleration path.

current_cached_jvp_speed_probe.scope: speed diagnostic only; this script uses the generic direct residual assembler and is not comparable to attached-policy residual closure

followup37_fastpath.assessment: The frame-force fastpath makes 480-column residual-only finite-difference correction cheap enough to keep exploring, but the accepted alpha remains tiny.

followup38_fastpath.assessment: The 600-column correction still descends, but the improvement tapered again; most remaining wall time is now setup and repeated candidate residual replay, not finite-difference matrix construction.

followup39_shell_bending_fastpath.assessment: A shell-bending/drilling-targeted pass still descended at roughly the followup38 rate, but the dominant hotspot stayed near the same shell/frame cancellation region.

followup40_shell_normal_fastpath.assessment: Targeting shell-normal rows produced only a 1.06e-6 N accepted descent, confirming that the current residual-row correction family is tapering too hard to reach the gate by repetition.

followup41_shell_bending_batch_alpha.assessment: The row-correction driver now reuses the residual-only batch path for alpha replay. It removed the duplicate per-alpha residual evaluation path, but the accepted descent stayed near 1.04e-6 N and wall time is still dominated by initial assembly plus finite-difference support-column work.

followup42_shell_bending_batch_fd.assessment: The row-correction driver now batches finite-difference support-column perturbations as well as alpha candidates. FD replay was reduced to 12 batches / 627 states with cache reuse across support sweeps, but the accepted descent collapsed to 1.30e-7 N, proving that this residual-row correction family is no longer a practical path to the gate by repetition.

followup43_global_krylov.assessment: A current-tangent-preconditioned global Krylov direction with wide alpha search reopened descent after row-correction saturation. The accepted improvement is 2.77e-5 N, over 200x larger than followup42's FD-batched row correction.

followup44_global_krylov_no_descent.assessment: Repeating the same global Krylov configuration after the followup43 hotspot shift did not descend. The next pass needs retuned scaling/preconditioning or a shell-membrane/coupled material direction rather than blind repetition.

followup45_46_scaled_floor_global_krylov.assessment: Residual row-scale floor retuning breaks the followup44 no-descent state, but the repeat pass collapses to 6.78e-9 N improvement. It is useful as a direction diagnostic, not as a gate-closure loop.

followup46_hotspot_jvp_consistency.assessment: Followup46 hotspot tangent/FD JVP is consistent to numerical precision, while frame-diagonal Newton candidates worsen the residual. The blocker is coupled direction/globalization, not a local Jacobian mismatch.

followup47_49_coupled_row_nodeblock.assessment: Coupled largest-row node-block current-tangent correction reopened a stronger local lane: followup47 improved 3.40e-5 N and followup48 improved 1.70e-5 N from the followup46 frontier. A narrowed followup49 repeat with the same top-4/support16 shape did not descend, so the lane is live but needs adaptive retargeting/support refresh after each frontier transition.

followup47_51_coupled_row_nodeblock.assessment: Coupled largest-row node-block current-tangent correction remains the strongest live G1 lane in this sequence. It moved the attached-policy residual from 0.0340294701 to 0.0336466411 N. Narrow fixed support failed at followup49, but broad adaptive target/support refresh recovered large descent at refresh followup49 and followup50. Followup51 then no-descended, followup52 residual-node-block retargeting only found numerical-noise improvement, and followup53/54 showed shell element blocks and shell rotation-only rows do not descend. Followup55-57 geometry-normal shell translation retargeting reopened descent, and followup58 adds a new coupled geometry-normal plus bending/drilling target mode. Followup59 still descended, followup60 finite-difference row-submatrix did not, followup61 broad retargeting only found numerical-floor descent, followup62 scaled-floor global Krylov improved only 1.36e-8 N, and followup63 secant-family history globalization worsened the residual. The latest frontier is shell_membrane-dominant again, so the next pass needs a higher-level coupled shell/material formulation or production backend lane rather than more local row-correction/Krylov/secant repetition.

followup342_361_total_scaled_component_f.assessment: Residual-node-block and shell-geometry-normal+bending retargets no-descended from followup341. Total-component-magnitude scaling reopened two small component-FD descents, and HIP full residual broad top96 replay remained positive, but the 342-361 sequence only removed about 2.70e-6 N. This lane is useful as a numerically safe frontier extender, not a practical gate-closure strategy by repetition.

followup362_365_attached_equilibrium_new.assessment: Once focused equilibrium Newton is run under attached_components_only, a single regularized-direct Newton step lowers the current attached-policy residual from 0.033136466982157 N to 1.787703126865381e-06 N, passing the 0.001 N residual gate.

torch_rocm.assessment: Torch ROCm is installed but does not currently expose the RX 6900 XT as a cuda device, so Torch sparse/lstsq is not a usable acceleration path in this environment without runtime repair.

native_hip_smokes.assessment: Native HIP kernel execution is available and fast enough to justify a custom residual/JVP replay engine.

rust_engine_scope.current_scope: story-level nonlinear frame / hook and GPU Newton contract support
rust_engine_scope.assessment: Rust is useful as the stable FFI/orchestration layer, but the existing Rust path is not yet the full MGT frame-shell residual replay engine.

frame_internal_force.current_shape: Python loop over frame_elements with per-element 12x12 force recovery and scatter-add
frame_internal_force.acceleration_target: prepacked element arrays plus Rust/HIP element-parallel force recovery and deterministic scatter/reduction

shell_internal_force.current_shape: cached shell operator followed by CSR matvec K_shell @ u
shell_internal_force.acceleration_target: batched CSR matvec for multiple perturbed/candidate u vectors, or sparse target-row JVP extraction without full residual replay

row_correction_driver.current_shape: finite-difference support columns and alpha candidates both replay through the residual-only batch internal-force path
row_correction_driver.acceleration_target: move the batch residual/JVP replay and current-tangent-preconditioned global Krylov lane into a persistent Rust/HIP worker, then retune around the shell-membrane hotspot that appears after followup43
row_correction_driver.adaptive_global_krylov_controller_update: run_mgt_direct_residual_adaptive_preconditioned_global_newton.py now passes residual_scale_floor to child global Krylov probes so scaled-floor retuning can be swept without manual one-off commands.

validation.native_hip_frame_force_batch_assessment: The native HIP frame-force batch bridge now runs when the process has GPU device access. It provides a 129x kernel-level speedup over the CPU prepacked frame batch on followup40 with relative force error 7.1e-12. This is real but insufficient by itself because the latest residual frontier is dominated by shell bending/drilling and shell membrane rows.
validation.native_hip_shell_csr_batch_assessment: The native HIP shell CSR batch bridge now validates the dominant shell path directly on followup40. The kernel is 2.69x faster than SciPy CSR batch matvec for 64 states with 4.7e-15 relative force error. Subprocess and host-copy overhead still dominate, so the next engineering step is in-process residual replay batching or a persistent Rust/HIP worker rather than one subprocess per candidate batch.
validation.post_shell_hip_full_residual_composed_as: Followup65 composes native HIP frame-force and shell-CSR outputs with the CPU spring term into a full residual batch and residual-difference JVP contract at the post-shell frontier. It passes against the CPU batch residual with 1.162e-6 N absolute residual difference, 8.58e-12 relative residual difference, and 1.72e-15 relative JVP difference. The residual gate is still open because the composed base residual is 0.0336384785 N, and this remains subprocess-composed rather than a persistent in-process Rust/HIP worker.
validation.post_shell_hip_full_residual_single_boun: Followup66 upgrades followup65 from host-composed component replay into a single native HIP subprocess boundary. Frame scatter, shell CSR, spring CSR, external-load subtraction, and free-DOF residual output all execute behind one HIP bridge. It passes against CPU batch residual/JVP with the same numerical agreement as followup65 and improves estimated kernel speedup from 5.43x to 12.02x. The next production step is persistent in-process Rust/HIP residency, not another subprocess wrapper.
validation.post_shell_hip_newton_row_backend_assess: Followup67 connects the followup66 native HIP full residual backend to the direct residual Newton row-correction FD/alpha batch replay boundary. This moves the backend from isolated replay evidence into an actual correction driver path. The tiny largest-row smoke accepted a 3.62e-7 N descent, but the residual gate remains open at 0.0336381164 N.
validation.post_shell_hip_largest_rows_followup69_a: Followup69 uses the connected HIP full-residual row backend on a broader largest-row finite-difference row-correction search. It accepts two promotions and reduces the attached-policy direct residual by 2.10e-5 N, from 0.0336384785 N to 0.0336175176 N. Followup68 shows the broader shell geometry-normal+bending target remains no-descent at this frontier.
validation.post_shell_hip_largest_rows_followup70_7: Followup70-72 continue from the followup69 checkpoint with refreshed HIP-backed largest-row finite-difference row correction. They accept 18 promotions over three probes and reduce the attached-policy direct residual by 1.50e-4 N, from 0.0336175176 N to 0.0334674359 N. The followup71 component frontier remains mixed shell_membrane, shell_bending_drilling, and frame, so this is live descent evidence rather than final closure.
validation.post_shell_hip_largest_rows_followup73_7: Followup73 squeezes the support=1 largest-row tail by only 1.90e-6 N and then no-descends. Followup74 tries support 4/8 from the followup73 state and no-descends immediately, with the best candidate worsening to 0.034802 N. The latest component frontier is still a flat mixed frame/shell_membrane/shell_bending_drilling plateau, so plain largest_rows row correction is saturated.
validation.post_shell_hip_coupled_block_followup75_: Followup75 tests residual_node_blocks with node-block support after largest_rows saturation and only accepts a numerical-floor 5.08e-8 N improvement before no-descent. Followup76 tests residual_element_blocks with neighbor expansion, residual-weighted support, node-block support, and signed alphas; it also no-descends. The current row-target family is saturated for the mixed frame/shell_membrane/shell_bending_drilling plateau.
validation.post_shell_hip_frontier_component_follow: A new frontier_component_rows target mode now loads residual_component_breakdown.top_rows from a frontier probe and component-balances frame, shell_membrane, and shell_bending_drilling target rows. Followup77 proves the hook works but no-descends. Followup78 adds SVD condition capping and also no-descends. The next step is not another target-row wrapper; it is component-scaled direction construction and/or persistent Rust/HIP residual/JVP replay for wider signed searches.
validation.post_shell_hip_frontier_component_scaled: Followup79 adds dominant-component row-equation scaling and followup80 adds total-component row-equation scaling for frontier_component_rows. Both no-descend from the followup75 checkpoint. Component-balanced target selection plus component-magnitude equation scaling is still not enough to reopen descent at the mixed frame/shell plateau.
validation.post_shell_global_krylov_post_component_: Followup81 retries current-tangent-preconditioned global Krylov with residual-diagonal displacement scaling from the followup75 checkpoint. It finds only a 5.15e-11 N numerical-floor descent and does not promote, so the current scaled global Krylov variant is also saturated at this plateau.
validation.post_shell_cached_component_block_basis_: Followup82-84 add a cached residual/JVP component-block basis path, fix the cached probe load-path contract so it can run against attached_components_only, and test dominant-component, component+dof, and gate-limited-alpha variants from the followup75 checkpoint. None promotes: the best observed descent is only 1.59e-12 N, so current component-block basis construction is also saturated at the 0.03346548 N plateau.
validation.post_shell_residual_jacobian_followup85_: Followup85 shows the plateau is a broad mixed frame/shell cancellation state with consistent component reconstruction and local tangent projection. Followup86 shows assembled tangent actions match finite-difference residual JVPs to near machine precision for checked global and hotspot directions, so the immediate blocker is not a first-order tangent/FD mismatch.
validation.post_shell_cached_top96_active_set_follo: Broad top96 active-set cached residual/JVP correction reopens a small live descent after component-block basis saturation. Followup87-89 accept three promotions and lower the attached-policy residual to 0.0334649068 N, while followup90 drops below the 1e-8 promotion floor. The next solver move should be a minimax/Chebyshev active-set objective, larger active sets, or better scaling across mixed frame/shell cancellation rows.
validation.post_shell_cached_multi_ridge_followup91: Followup91-94 show pure selected-row Chebyshev/minimax cancellation is not a safe globalization path at this frontier: it can satisfy local linear active rows while worsening the nonlinear global residual. Followup95-97 add multi-ridge LS direction sweep against the same cached JVP matrix and reopen a repeatable descent lane, lowering the residual by 1.51e-6 N to 0.0334633965 N. The gate remains open and the rate is still too slow for closure by repetition.
validation.post_shell_cached_multi_ridge_followup98: Followup98-101 continue the multi-ridge lane from followup97. Followup98 is floor-near, but followup99 and followup101 recover larger 2e-6 N-class descents, lowering the latest residual to 0.0334587801 N. This is live descent evidence but still far from the 0.001 N residual gate.
validation.post_shell_cached_multi_ridge_followup10: Followup102-105 continue the fixed top96 multi-ridge sweep. Followup102 gives another 2e-6 N-class descent, followup104 drops near the floor, and followup105 recovers to a 5e-7 N-class descent. The lane is still live but sawtoothed, so adaptive ridge/alpha scheduling is the next controller step.

A new multi-ridge controller now chains the cached residual/JVP probe from promoted checkpoints. Followup106-108 all promote through the controller and lower the residual to 0.0334510584 N. This proves the controller works and the lane remains live, but it still has a long margin to the 0.001 N gate.

Followup109-111 all promote through the controller, lowering the residual to 0.0334470050 N. Followup110 and followup111 retain 1.5e-6 to 2.0e-6 N-class descent, so the controller-backed multi-ridge lane remains live.
validation.post_shell_cached_multi_ridge_followup11: Followup112-114 all promote through the controller, lowering the residual to 0.0334447940 N. Followup112 uses a negative-alpha ridge=0.1 direction, and followup113-114 use ridge=1e-4 directions, showing the best ridge/alpha remains frontier-dependent.

Followup115-117 all promote through the controller, lowering the residual to 0.0334422339 N. The best directions continue to vary across high-ridge negative alpha, ridge=1e-6, and ridge=1e-4 choices, reinforcing the need for adaptive scheduling rather than a single fixed ridge.

Followup118-120 use a denser alpha grid around the previously active 6.10e-5 and 1.53e-5 steps. All three promote and lower the residual to 0.0334382717 N; followup119 recovers another 2e-6 N-class descent. Denser alpha sampling is useful but still insufficient as a complete gate-closure strategy.
validation.post_shell_cached_multi_ridge_followup12: The cached residual/JVP probe can request the native HIP full-residual batch backend, but this workstation run falls back because the HIP binary reports no ROCm-capable device detected. The code path is wired; device execution still requires a visible ROCm GPU runtime.

Followup121-126 add safe residual-batch replay for alpha candidates while keeping finite-difference JVP replay on the single-state path. All six safe steps promote and lower the residual to 0.0334167342 N. The unsafe batch-JVP smoke shows tiny batch roundoff can destabilize the current ill-conditioned LS direction, so batch-JVP remains opt-in until a stronger equivalence guard/globalization is added.
Followup127-130 all promote through safe alpha-only CPU batch replay. The first step is near-floor, but followup129-130 recover 2e-6 N-class descents and lower the residual to 0.0334110017 N.
validation.post_shell_cached_multi_ridge_followup13: Followup131-134 all promote. The sawtooth pattern persists: followup132 and followup134 are floor-near, while followup133 recovers a 4.03e-6 N descent. This supports adaptive scheduling around ridge/alpha changes rather than terminating the lane at the first weak step.

Followup135-142 all promote under safe alpha-only CPU batch replay. The lane remains live: followup138 recovers a 4.18e-6 N descent and followup140 recovers a 2.64e-6 N descent after smaller moves. Latest residual is 0.0333918104 N.
validation.post_shell_cached_multi_ridge_followup14: Followup143-150 all promote. Followup143 and followup145 recover 4e-6 N-class descents, but followup148-150 weaken to sub-5e-7 N moves. Latest residual is 0.0333785854 N; the lane remains live but is again approaching a weak-step region.
validation.post_shell_cached_followup151_165_assess: Followup151 shows component-dof redistribution alone only yields a 5.99e-8 N final descent. A shell_membrane-only support3 branch from that checkpoint no-promotes and worsens. The shell_bending_drilling support3 branch reopens descent, and broad top96 multi-ridge replay after that perturbation promotes through followup165, with followup159, followup161, and followup164 each recovering about 8.1e-6 N descents. Latest residual is 0.0333391507 N.
validation.post_shell_cached_followup166_173_assess: Followup166-173 all promote from the followup165 shell-bending-refreshed frontier. The lane remains live but is sawtoothing: followup166, 168, 171, and 172 recover 2e-6 to 4.1e-6 N descents, while followup167, 169, 170, and 173 are weaker. Latest residual is 0.0333244006 N.
validation.post_shell_cached_followup174_180_assess: Followup174-175 intentionally checks the weak-step guard and confirms two floor-near broad top96 promotions. Followup176 shell_bending_drilling refresh is itself small, but it perturbs the active set enough for followup177-180 broad top96 replay to recover a 1.63e-5 N descent at followup178 and lower the latest residual to 0.0333015220 N.
validation.post_shell_cached_followup181_190_assess: Followup181-188 all promote from the followup180 refresh-recovered frontier and lower residual to 0.0332789791 N. Followup188 is weak, but followup189-190 recover two 1.02e-6 N-class moves, so the two-consecutive-weak-step shell_bending refresh trigger is not met. Latest residual is 0.0332769479 N.
validation.post_shell_cached_followup191_199_assess: Followup191-198 all promoted but only reduced the attached-policy direct residual by 3.58593740657e-06 N; followup197 and followup198 were consecutive weak steps below 6e-7 N. A shell_bending_drilling support3 refresh from followup198 no-promoted, top128 expansion worsened conditioning and only found a 4e-9 N diagnostic descent, and frame-only focus no-descended. The best followup199 diagnostic was component_dof block-basis, specifically component_block:frame:rx, but it still only found a 1.4e-7 N descent. Selected-row projection coverage is already near 1.0, so the bottleneck is not missing selected-row coverage; it is tiny accepted alpha / trust scaling, ill-conditioned broad LS directions, and a need for block-aware or global tangent globalization rather than blind top-k promotion.
validation.post_shell_cached_followup191_199_retent: Capacity-aware retention: followup191-198 checkpoint/probe NPZ artifacts were pruned after JSON extraction. The archived continuation checkpoint remains followup190; followup191-199 is retained as plateau/strategy evidence only.
validation.post_shell_followup200_guarded_assessmen: Followup200 adds a capacity-safe checkpoint floor and proves it on the retained followup190 frontier. The component-block frame:rx diagnostic again finds only a below-floor 1.40e-7 N improvement and writes no checkpoint. A one-factor current-tangent global Krylov guard no-descends: its best trial worsens the residual to 0.0345169949 N. The retained continuation frontier therefore remains followup190, while future G1 probes should require material descent before writing large NPZ state.

implemented_followup.assessment: The first practical G1 replay acceleration is implemented for reference-geometry frame force recovery, and a CPU batch residual-only replay contract now validates frame/shell/spring candidate replay against the sequential evaluator. The native HIP frame-force batch bridge now runs with GPU device access and gives about 129x kernel-level speedup over CPU frame replay on followup40. The native HIP shell CSR batch bridge also runs and gives 2.69x kernel-level speedup over CPU shell batch replay on the 1.90M-nnz followup40 shell matrix. Followup41 wires row-correction alpha replay into the batch internal-force evaluator, followup42 wires finite-difference support-column replay into the same batch path, and followup43 proves a stronger current-tangent-preconditioned global Krylov direction can still move the physical residual. The remaining high-impact work is to make that global/coupled lane persistent and retune it after hotspot transitions, not to keep widening the saturated row-correction family. Followup45/46 add residual-scale-floor retuning for the global Krylov lane: it proves the followup44 no-descent state can be escaped, but the repeat improvement saturates immediately, leaving the next high-impact work in a coupled shell bending/membrane/frame or material Newton direction. Followup47/48 show that coupled largest-row node-block current-tangent correction can still produce useful direct-residual descent after scaled-floor Krylov saturation; followup49 shows fixed retargeting saturates and the next step is adaptive support/target refresh. Followup47-50 show coupled largest-row node-block correction with broad target/support refresh is now the strongest residual descent lane; followup51 no-descent shows it still needs adaptive retargeting/globalization rather than fixed repetition. Followup191-199 now show the cached top96 lane is weak-step saturated at 0.0332733620 N; selected-row coverage is already near complete, so the next useful work is block-aware/global tangent trust scaling rather than more blind top-k cached-JVP promotion. Capacity retention was tightened: followup191-199 large NPZ checkpoints are not retained because they document weak-step saturation rather than a material new continuation state. Followup200 adds a material-improvement checkpoint floor and validates JSON-only weak-step retention; the tested component-block and current-tangent global Krylov guards do not create a new continuation frontier.

advances.assessment: The fastpath made a 360-column element-block finite-difference row correction cheap enough to run, reopening descent beyond the prior support168 no-descent probe.
A second fastpath wide element-block pass still descended but with a smaller accepted alpha, indicating useful but tapering residual reduction.
Expanding the element-block search to 480 support columns still produced a gate-eligible descent, but the top hotspot stayed on node 2202 rx.
A 600-column follow-up remained numerically useful but tapered further, confirming that engine speed helps iteration throughput while the correction family still needs a stronger coupled direction.
Shell-bending/drilling targeting produced another small descent, but the improvement stayed near 5e-6 N and did not break the node 2202 shell/frame cancellation frontier.
Shell-normal targeting descended by only 1.06e-6 N, so the latest pattern is too slow for brute-force repeated CPU probes.

Batch alpha replay is now integrated and proven in the row-correction driver, but the residual descent remained tiny. The next speed target is FD support-column/JVP batching, not more alpha replay tuning.

Batch FD replay is now integrated and proven in the row-correction driver, with cache reuse reducing later support sweeps to mostly cache hits. The accepted descent fell to 1.30e-7 N, so the next G1 work must change the coupled correction direction rather than widen this row-correction family.

Current-tangent-preconditioned global Krylov with a wider alpha search reopened useful residual descent after row-correction saturation. It moved the frontier hotspot from node 2202 rx shell bending/drilling to node 2377 uz shell membrane.
Repeat scaled-floor global Krylov accepted, but improvement collapsed to 6.78e-9 N; the lane is diagnostic and saturated.
Second accepted coupled row-nodeblock pass. Improvement remained live at 1.70e-5 N, but followup49 with the same narrowed support no longer descended.
Second broad refresh chunk accepted two promotions and improved the residual by 1.62e-4 N; followup51 then no-descended, requiring another adaptive retargeting strategy.

Residual-node-block retargeting after followup51 produced only numerical-noise improvement and wrote no checkpoint. The next G1 probe should change to shell element/physics-coupled targets or use FD row-submatrix checks on a refreshed support.

Post-followup51 retargeting shows plain node blocks, shell element blocks, and isolated shell bending/drilling rows are not enough. Geometry-normal shell translation rows do descend, and the new coupled geometry-normal plus bending/drilling target mode also descended. Followup59 tapered, followup60 finite-difference row-submatrix did not descend, and followup61 found only numerical-floor improvement, so this local row-correction family is now saturated at 0.0336384921 N.

After the coupled shell lane tapered, finite-difference row-submatrix, scaled-floor global Krylov, and accepted-history secant-family globalization did not reopen a material descent. The next work should move the coupled shell membrane/bending correction into a higher-level material/globalization formulation or a faster production residual/JVP backend, not repeat local row/history sweeps.

latest_frontier_pattern.assessment: The last component frontier probe before HIP-backed row descent is followup62: node 2379 uz and node 2676 uz shell_membrane rows lead, with node 2202 rx shell_bending_drilling close behind. Followup69 has since lowered the scalar residual through HIP-backed largest-row correction; a refreshed component frontier probe is the next diagnostic before choosing the next target family.

latest_only_followup201_248.hip_backend_note: HIP full-residual backend was requested in followup201-232 but this sandbox reported no ROCm-capable device, so those probes fell back to single residual replay. followup233-248 used CPU batch alpha replay with zero fallback.

component_refresh_followup249_258.assessment: Followup249-250 component-frontier finite-difference correction reopened descent but weakened quickly; followup251-258 broad top96 cached multi-ridge replay was faster and advanced the retained frontier to followup258.

latest_only_followup259_274.assessment: Followup259-274 broad top96 cached multi-ridge replay remained live and advanced the retained frontier by 1.02e-5 N. The current frontier remains a mixed shell_membrane/shell_bending/frame cancellation state and the residual gate remains open.

latest_only_followup275_290.assessment: Followup275-290 broad top96 cached multi-ridge replay remained live and advanced the retained frontier by 1.28e-5 N, with 2e-6 N-class recovery steps after weak moves. The current frontier remains a mixed shell_membrane/shell_bending/frame cancellation state and the residual gate remains open.

latest_only_followup291_306.assessment: Followup291-306 broad top96 cached multi-ridge replay advanced the retained frontier by 1.20e-5 N. Early 2e-6 N-class moves show the lane still works, but the weak followup306 tail suggests another component refresh if the next continuation starts weak.

latest_only_followup307_322.assessment: Followup307-322 broad top96 cached multi-ridge replay remained positive but weakened to only 3.11e-6 N total improvement. The next G1 step should use the refreshed followup322 component frontier for component/frontier FD refresh rather than another long blind broad continuation.

component_fd_hip_followup323_341.assessment: Followup323 component-frontier finite-difference correction with HIP full-residual batch replay reopened a useful 1.96e-6 N descent. Followup324 and followup333 weakened below 1.3e-7 N. The followup325-332 broad cached multi-ridge replay was the strongest post-refresh lane at 1.49e-5 N, while followup334-341 tapered to 1.50e-6 N. The next G1 step should not keep blindly extending broad top96; it should use the retained followup341 component frontier to test a different block/global tangent lane or retuned target family.

decision.expected_best_use: custom HIP/Rust residual replay and batched JVP engine, especially shell CSR batch matvec plus target-row residual metrics, not another sparse solver or Torch least-squares wrapper
decision.reason: Followup47-50 show the active G1 direction is coupled largest-row node-block correction with refreshed target/support sets, not single-row scaling or fixed Krylov repetition. Followup55-59 further show geometry-normal shell translation rows and a coupled shell geometry-normal plus bending/drilling target can reopen descent after nodeblock and shell-element no-descent. Followup60-63 show the local coupled row-correction, finite-difference row-submatrix, current scaled-floor global Krylov, and accepted-history secant variants have now saturated. Followup64 revalidates the production backend lane at the post-shell frontier: CPU residual batch replay, HIP shell CSR batch, and HIP frame-force batch all pass. Followup65 composes those HIP component outputs into a full residual/JVP contract with 5.43x estimated kernel speedup over CPU batch. Followup66 upgrades that into a single native HIP full residual/JVP subprocess boundary with 1.162e-6 N residual difference, 8.58e-12 relative residual difference, 1.72e-15 relative JVP difference, and 12.02x kernel speedup over CPU batch. Followup67 wires that backend into the direct residual Newton row-correction FD/alpha batch replay path and accepts a small 3.62e-7 N descent. Followup68 confirms the broader shell geometry-normal+bending target is still no-descent. Followup69 uses the connected HIP backend on largest-row finite-difference correction and accepts two promotions, lowering residual by 2.10e-5 N to 0.0336175176 N. Full closure still requires adaptive coupled shell material/globalization plus a persistent in-process Rust/HIP residual/JVP worker, not just subprocess kernels.

rough_extrapolation_from_followup32.assessment: This extrapolation is optimistic because followup34 already showed no descent; it shows why algorithmic direction and replay throughput both matter.

rough_extrapolation_from_latest_fastpath.assessment: The coupled row-nodeblock refresh lane is materially stronger than scaled-floor Krylov, but followup51 shows it still reaches local no-descent plateaus. Closure likely needs an adaptive controller that changes target/support family after no-descent and then ports residual replay to production ROCm/HIP.

recommended_next_path: Close the remaining G1 proof gap by recording/verifying the followup365 increment gate or adding a focused increment-gate receipt, then port the attached-policy Newton solve off CPU regularized_direct into the native ROCm/HIP production lane; G7 remains next after G1 evidence hardening.
claim_boundary: This assessment proves engine availability and identifies active G1 descent lanes. It does not claim G1 residual closure. HIP frame replay and shell CSR replay are validated as isolated kernels, followup65 validates a subprocess-composed full residual/JVP batch contract, followup66 validates a single native HIP full residual/JVP subprocess boundary, followup67 validates direct Newton row-correction integration, followup69 validates live descent through that backend, followup70-72 validate continued refreshed largest-row descent to 0.0334674359 N, followup73-74 validate saturation of the plain largest_rows branch at 0.0334655321 N, followup75-76 validate saturation of the existing node/element block row-target family at 0.0334654813 N, followup77-78 validate the new frontier_component_rows hook while showing that target selection alone does not reopen descent, followup79-80 show component-magnitude equation scaling also no-descends, followup81 shows current scaled global Krylov is at numerical-floor descent, followup82-84 show current cached component-block residual/JVP basis construction is also numerical-floor only, followup85-86 show the plateau is a consistent mixed frame/shell cancellation state rather than a checked tangent/JVP mismatch, followup87-90 show broad top96 active-set cached residual/JVP descent reopens only a small three-promotion improvement to 0.0334649068 N before saturating, followup91-94 show selected-row Chebyshev/minimax cancellation is not a safe globalization path, followup95-97 show multi-ridge active-set descent lowers the residual to 0.0334633965 N, followup98-101 extend the same lane to 0.0334587801 N, followup102-105 extend it to 0.0334556552 N, followup106-108 validate the controller path down to 0.0334510584 N, followup109-111 continue it to 0.0334470050 N, followup112-114 continue it to 0.0334447940 N, followup115-117 continue it to 0.0334422339 N, followup118-120 continue it with dense alpha sampling to 0.0334382717 N, followup121-126 continue it with safe alpha-batch replay to 0.0334167342 N, followup127-134 continue it to 0.0334045911 N, followup135-142 continue it to 0.0333918104 N, followup143-150 continue it to 0.0333785854 N, followup151-165 continue it through a shell_bending_drilling refresh plus broad top96 replay to 0.0333391507 N, followup166-173 continue the same lane to 0.0333244006 N, followup174-180 validate weak-step refresh and lower it to 0.0333015220 N, and followup181-190 continue broad top96 replay to 0.0332769479 N. Batched row correction, global Krylov, scaled-floor retuning, hotspot JVP consistency, coupled row-nodeblock descent, component-block JVP basis diagnostics, physical residual/Jacobian audit, top96 active-set descent, minimax negative evidence, multi-ridge positive evidence, multi-ridge controller automation, weak-step shell_bending refresh, and cached residual/JVP alpha-batch replay are integrated. Adaptive material/shell/frame Newton, full-load path closure, residual gate pass, batch-JVP equivalence guarding, and production in-process ROCm/HIP residual/JVP residency remain open.

checkpoint_retention_policy.note: Followup365 keeps only the attached-policy residual-gate checkpoint. The all-components diagnostic checkpoint and superseded followup361 checkpoint are pruned after compact scalar extraction.
